title: Project Capstone — Build & Code
subtitle: Stop Planning, Start Building — Let's Code It!
overview: Students move from planning to action — writing actual Python code for their capstone GUI project. This session focuses on setting up the project structure, building the main window, adding widgets, and wiring up event handlers. Emphasis is on learning by doing and debugging confidently.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — student screens with code, debugging moments, finished GUI windows, whiteboard code walkthroughs.

keyTopics: Tkinter Boilerplate — import tkinter, create window, mainloop()
Setting Window Title and Size — title() and geometry()
Adding Widgets — Label, Button, Entry, Frame
Layout with grid() — rows and columns for clean UI
Writing Functions — def on_click(): and connecting to command=
Reading User Input — Entry.get() to capture typed text
Debugging — Reading error messages and fixing them confidently
Project Structure — Keeping code clean and organised
Stretch Goals — Adding colours, fonts, and extra features

activity.title: Build Your Capstone GUI App
parts: Notebook for tracking bugs and fixes
steps: Open VS Code and create a new file — app.py
Write the Tkinter boilerplate: import, create window, mainloop()
Set your window title and size using title() and geometry()
Add your first widget — a Label with your app's name
Add an Entry box for user input
Add a Button and write a function it will call on click
Use Entry.get() inside your function to read user input
Test, find bugs, read the error message, fix it — repeat!
Stretch: Add colours with bg= and fg=, change fonts with font=

quiz.q: What are the 3 essential lines every Tkinter program must have?
options: import tkinter, create window with Tk(), call mainloop()
quiz.explanation: Every Tkinter app needs: import tkinter → window = tkinter.Tk() → window.mainloop(). These 3 lines create and keep your window open!

quiz.q: Which line sets the title shown in the window's title bar?
quiz.explanation: window.title('My App') sets the title bar text! It's usually one of the first things you write after creating the window.

quiz.q: Which line sets the size of a Tkinter window to 800 wide and 600 tall?
quiz.explanation: window.geometry('800x600') sets width x height in pixels. The format is always 'WIDTHxHEIGHT' as a string.

quiz.q: How do you create a Label widget that says 'Hello!' in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: Label(window, text='Hello!').pack() creates a label and places it on the window. The parent is always the window, and you must call a layout method like pack() or grid()!

quiz.q: Which layout method places widgets in rows and columns like a table?
quiz.explanation: grid() places widgets at specific row= and column= positions — perfect for forms, calculators, and structured layouts. Example: button.grid(row=0, column=1)

quiz.q: How do you read the text a user typed into an Entry widget called 'entry'?
quiz.explanation: entry.get() returns whatever the user typed as a string. You'll use this inside your button function to grab the input and do something with it!

quiz.q: How do you update a Label's text after the app is running?
quiz.explanation: label.config(text='New text') updates the label dynamically! You'll call this inside a function to show results after a button click.

quiz.q: What does the command= parameter in a Button do?
options: Links the button to a function that runs on click
quiz.explanation: command= connects your button to a function! Example: Button(window, text='Click Me', command=my_function) — when clicked, my_function() runs automatically.

quiz.q: You see this error: 'NameError: name my_function is not defined'. What went wrong?
options: The function was called before it was defined in the code
quiz.explanation: NameError means Python can't find the function! Always define your def my_function(): BEFORE creating the button that uses it. Order matters in Python!

quiz.q: What is the correct way to define a function called on_click in Python?
quiz.explanation: def on_click(): is correct Python syntax! Don't forget the colon at the end and indent the code inside the function.

quiz.q: Which parameter changes the background colour of a widget in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: bg= sets the background colour! Example: Button(window, text='Go', bg='green', fg='white') — bg is background, fg is foreground (text colour).

quiz.q: What should you do FIRST when you see a Python error message?
options: Delete all your code and start again
Read the last line of the error — it tells you what went wrong
Ask someone else to fix it
quiz.explanation: Read the error! The last line tells you the error type and message. The line above tells you exactly which line caused it. Bugs are clues — not failures!

quiz.q: What does pack() do to a widget?
options: Saves the widget to a file
Places the widget on the window automatically
quiz.explanation: pack() places the widget on the window! Without calling pack(), grid(), or place(), your widget exists in memory but won't appear on screen.

quiz.q: Which of these is a good habit when building a capstone project?
options: Write all 200 lines of code before testing
Build and test one small piece at a time
Use only one widget for everything
quiz.explanation: Build small, test often! Add one widget or function, run the code, check it works, then move to the next. This makes bugs much easier to find and fix.

quiz.q: How do you change the font of a Label in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: Use the font= parameter with a tuple: font=('Arial', 14) sets font family and size. You can also add 'bold' or 'italic': font=('Arial', 14, 'bold').
